evidence: "I stumbled upon the 1978 television movie The Nativity... this is a nice little Biblical tale."
"I didn't expect to see the airplane thriller genre to revive anytime soon after 9/11... Flightplan embraces the new less on action, more on brains turnabout."
"The alternating between Herod's madness, the journey of the Magi, and the eponymous drama is not a problem, but Herod is dropped all together halfway through the film."
"Multi-leveled storylines and ambiguous friends and foes are just a few of Sharpe's Battle's highlights."
"The Nativity also takes awhile to get going... the pace improves greatly."
"Sharpe's Siege is not perfect. There's too little and yet too much going on between characters old and new."
"The Spanish locations, however, look surprisingly ancient and Middle Eastern."
"The production values are on form here. This might have been one of the big budget episodes."
"Sean Bean returns in this 1994 telefilm as Richard Sharpe..."
"I took a brief break from reading, watching, and reviewing Sharpe, but soon enough I had to dive in again with Sharpe's Company."
"The beautiful English locales and majestic looks of the horses practically sell themselves."
"The stunningly beautiful yet violent and creepy cliffs and oceans onscreen add to the parental fear of the leads."
"The Nativity's biggest trouble is that it isn't as moving as it could be or spends more time on the exposition and fluff."
"The speeches from Connie and Mellors are so sincere, honest, and downright poetic that the audience can't help but root for the couple."

summary: The user enjoys a mix of historical dramas and thrillers, often appreciating strong emotional narratives and sensory experiences. They show a particular loyalty to Sean Bean's performances, valuing both production quality and narrative complexity in their viewing choices.
